title: Protopine

Chemical compound

**Protopine** is an [alkaloid](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") occurring in [opium poppy](/wiki/Opium_poppy "Opium poppy"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) *[Corydalis](/wiki/Corydalis "Corydalis")* tubers[[3]](#cite_note-3) and other plants of the family [papaveraceae](/wiki/Papaveraceae "Papaveraceae"), like *[Fumaria officinalis](/wiki/Fumaria_officinalis "Fumaria officinalis")*.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

It has been found to inhibit [histamine H1 receptors](/wiki/Histamine_H1_receptor "Histamine H1 receptor") and [platelet aggregation](/wiki/Platelet_aggregation "Platelet aggregation"), and acts as an [analgesic](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Biosynthesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protopine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biosynthesis")]

Protopine is derived in the plants such as the opium poppy, *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")*, from the [benzylisoquinoline alkaloid](/wiki/Benzylisoquinoline_alkaloids "Benzylisoquinoline alkaloids") (*S*)-[reticuline](/wiki/Reticuline "Reticuline") through a progressive series of five enzymatic transformations: 1) [berberine bridge enzyme](/wiki/BBE-like_enzymes "BBE-like enzymes") to [(*S*)-scoulerine](/wiki/Scoulerine "Scoulerine"); 2) [(*S*)-cheilanthifoline synthase](/wiki/(S)-cheilanthifoline_synthase "(S)-cheilanthifoline synthase")/CYP719A25 to [(*S*)-cheilanthifoline](/wiki/(S)-cheilanthifoline "(S)-cheilanthifoline"); 3)  [(*S*)-stylopine synthase](/wiki/(S)-stylopine_synthase "(S)-stylopine synthase")/CYP719A20 to (*S*)-[stylopine](/wiki/Stylopine "Stylopine"); 4) (*S*)-tetrahydroprotoberberine *N*-methyltransferase to (*S*)-*cis*-*N*-methylstylopine.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The final step is [oxidation](/wiki/Redox "Redox") by the enzyme [methyltetrahydroprotoberberine 14-monooxygenase](/wiki/Methyltetrahydroprotoberberine_14-monooxygenase "Methyltetrahydroprotoberberine 14-monooxygenase"):[[8]](#cite_note-NPR-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[![2D representation of the chemical structure of Q27105298.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/%28S%29-cis-N-methylstylopine.svg/250px-%28S%29-cis-N-methylstylopine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:(S)-cis-N-methylstylopine.svg)

(*S*)-*cis*-N-methylstylopine

+ [NADPH](/wiki/NADPH "NADPH")

O2

H2O

![Rightward reaction arrow with minor substrate(s) from top left and minor product(s) to top right](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[![2D representation of the chemical structure of protopine .](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Protopine_structure.svg/250px-Protopine_structure.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Protopine_structure.svg)

protopine

+ [NADP+](/wiki/NADP%2B "NADP+")

## Metabolism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Protopine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Metabolism")]

The enzyme [protopine 6-monooxygenase](/wiki/Protopine_6-monooxygenase "Protopine 6-monooxygenase") converts protopine into dihydrosanguinarine.[[8]](#cite_note-NPR-8)[[10]](#cite_note-10) NADP+ and water are the by-products.

[![2D representation of the chemical structure of protopine .](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Protopine_structure.svg/250px-Protopine_structure.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Protopine_structure.svg)

protopine

+ [NADPH](/wiki/NADPH "NADPH")

O2

H2O

![Rightward reaction arrow with minor substrate(s) from top left and minor product(s) to top right](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[![2D representation of the chemical structure of dihydrosanguinarine .](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Dihydrosanguinarine.png/250px-Dihydrosanguinarine.png)](/wiki/File:Dihydrosanguinarine.png)

[dihydrosanguinarine](/wiki/Dihydrosanguinarine "Dihydrosanguinarine")

+ [NADP+](/wiki/NADP%2B "NADP+")

The enzyme uses reduced [nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide_phosphate "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate") (NADPH), and oxygen, with the initial product being 6-hydroxyprotopine, which spontaneously forms dihydrosanguinarine, found in plants such as *[Sanguinaria canadensis](/wiki/Sanguinaria_canadensis "Sanguinaria canadensis")*.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
